sentences.text: My father came back about four months later, after being in prison for four months and--
In '41 already, yeah.
Is still winter, right.
Winter or spring.
And he was given a job in the kitchen as a cook, being that he was a restaurateur.

So that, however, they insisted that he has to, in order to keep that job, which was a good job-- it had to do with food-- he had to inform-- they wanted that he should inform on the other prisoners, what was going on in camp.
They-- well, whatever would be derogatory to the government or whatever, to the chief or whatever.
And, naturally, he refused.
I don't remember.
I remember my father came back, and I don't know what happened to the others.
I think they did come back.
No, he was really emaciated, very weak.
And I think this is one of the reasons why they let him out.
It was really bad.

Then he went, he worked in the kitchen for awhile, but he refused to inform on anybody or let everybody know not to talk in front of him, so he wouldn't have anything to-- and that got back to the [INAUDIBLE], the chief of the camp.
And as a punishment, he was taken off the job and sent into the woods to work in the forest also.
From sunrise to sunset.
There was no established hours.
We'd leave early in the morning and come back after the sun was down.
You couldn't see no more.
So in the wintertime, we were practically all night long, because night started, and it was like a long night.
On the other hand in the summer, it was all 20 hours of summer-- of daylight.
So we used to-- in the summer, we used to see the sun settling down and-- or rising in one place, and settling down just a few feet further 20 hours later, in the same spot.
They call these northern summers or whatever-- Northern Lights.
In any case, my father got sick in the forest also, and they let him come back to work in the kitchen sort of on a limited basis.
And that too helped us to sustain a little bit, because he would be able to give us a little more proportion and so on.
And that lasted until the Nazis invaded Russia, which was in 1941, towards the end of the summer, I believe.
Yeah, it must have been towards the end, because we were there all summer long.
And so they-- the Polish government in Russia decided to form a Polish brigade, the Kosciuszko Brigade-- Kosciuszko Brigade.
And because of that, they decided all Polish citizens are no longer enemies of the state and can be released from Siberia.
We found out afterwards that the whole Polish brigade, the Kosciuszko Brigade, was sent to the German front, and they were decimated right away.
They-- they-- a lot of them died on that-- on that front.
Well, not only from camp, all the Polish-- because there were a lot of Polish communists that lived in Russia.
And they formed that Polish brigade.
And therefore, they decided to release us from Siberia.
And so they permitted us to travel from Siberia.
They gave special permits of propusks, as they call it.
How do they spell it?
P-R-O-P-U-S-K.
Propusk.
It's a permit to travel.
Because you can't travel-- or you couldn't travel in Russia anywhere without a permit, from one place to another.
And so they allowed us to go down to middle Asia, which was warm country.
So to-- in our case was, to Kirghizia, the state Kirghizia.
And so they-- we went on a train that travelled south.
First of all-- oh, before we go there, we were able to load our belongings on our flatbeds, except my father.
We all got permits.
Because he was a troublemaker, they would not give him a permit.
And they would keep him there.
So my father managed to come with us till-- I think it was Asino, that other town that hooked up to the main railroad, the Tomsk.
There he says, [INAUDIBLE]-- children-- I know I'm not going to go with you.
You go, and I'll catch up with you somehow.
So he traveled without a permit up to Tomsk-- Tomsk or Asino, one of those two places.
And he was arrested by the KBG or the [INAUDIBLE].
And we hadn't heard from him then.
We traveled-- he was put, literally, in a prison over there.
For what reason, we still don't know.
I mean, just because he was a leader or whatever.
And so we--
We thought that was the end.
But we decided to go with the rest of the [INAUDIBLE] with the train.

Well, all of Middle Asia, it was they took Tushgan and Tajikistan, they wanted us from Serbia to a warm climate, which is the southern, Middle Asia, which is Kirghizia, Uzbekistan, Tajikistan, all those warm parts of the country.
And we went by train down there.
In the meantime, my sister decided to go off on one of the stops to get some hot water-- they always have hot water at all the stations-- to make some soup for my mother, whatever it was.
And the train just took off, and she was left at one of the places.
The next stop, my mother says to me, get off and go look for your sister.
So I got off, and I started inquiring whereabouts of hers.
And by sheer coincidence, on another stop we met up, the two of us.
And she was bedraggled.
Somehow she got into a train with coal-- they were transporting coal-- and she was all black, and how I recognized her, I don't know.
Maybe she recognized me.
But from there on, the two of us traveled together.
We couldn't get on a train, so we managed-- I schlepped her on-- to sit on a car load of lumber, tree trunks.
They were transporting it down south.
We sat between the two wagons or two trains.
No.
This was on a train.
And we knew we had to go-- we didn't know our destination.
We didn't know where we were going, the whole train load of people, but we knew we were going south.
So as we got up, got out at different places, we kept asking, where do the trains go to?
And at one point, a friend of ours, an acquaintance, told us that they heard that they're going to Belovodsk, Kirghizia.
Belovodsk is the name of the town near Frunza, which is the capital of Kirghizia.
Frunza is F-R-U-N-Z-A, Frunza, Frunzi.
And so we made our way, just the two of us, towards that destination.
How did we eat?
That's a good question.
I don't recall.
We must have had some rubles with us or something.
Yeah.
I don't recall that at all.

Oh, we spoke Russian by that time, because we spent-- while I was going to school in the Ukraine, so-- we finally got back to Belovodsk, and we found out that my mother and my brother and sister-in-law were sent to a collective farm in Belovodsk.
So we looked them up, and when we got there-- and my sister got sick.
She got, I think it was typhus, from all the dirt and all that.
And she got deathly sick.
And I got an assignment to work on the collective farm, drive a pair of-- a wagon with a pair of horses-- to deliver the wheat from one end to the town.
And we-- we were able to survive, literally, from the-- from what they gave us on the collective farm.
Plus, as I was driving the team of horses with wheat, I would tie up my bottoms of my pants and pour the-- as I was driving, pour the wheat into my pants to fill up the pants.
And I would drive by the little cabin that we stayed in and jump off and undo the bottoms of the pants, and we would accumulate wheat that way.
By that time we were informed that the Polish government in London--
In 1940, '41, '42, somewhere in-- because by that time, was all summer already, so I don't know seasons, exactly.
But the Polish government and London had an agreement with the Soviet Union to form a Polish army, the Anders army, A-N-D-E-R-S.
And because of that, they became very lenient with our people.
And the Polish army, the Anders army that they formed, decided to go to Iran, and they went through-- as a matter of fact, they jumped off-- a lot of our people jumped off in Iran and went to Israel.
And [?
Bagan ?] was one of the people that was with us.
Yeah.
He was in Middle Asia.
He was in Siberia and then in Middle Asia.
Yeah.
He was one-- he joined the Anders army, and when they-- I understand when they got to Iran, the whole bunch of them just separated and went to Palestine.
No.
No.
He wasn't in our camp, but I know that he was in that--
No, no.
He was just in that same area.
No.
It was one of the-- one of the people in our type of situation.
And of course, I was too young to go to an army, but although, they caught me in Kirghizia-- in Belovodsk.
They took me to a labor camp.
They called it the Trudovoi Front, the labor front-- to work for the-- for whatever it is.
I never got to work for them, because when we got to camp, they gave out the rations, the booklets for bread.
And I collected the whole week's worth or two weeks worth, and I just put in my work sack and left.
From that point on, I was illegal.
And the many times they would come and look for me in Belovodsk.
And one time, I came very close.
They came looking for-- I was literally a deserter from the labor front.
And my mother dressed me up as a girl, and they came in, they're looking for men and young men.
My mother says only my little daughter is here.
And I was supposedly in bed with a [INAUDIBLE] and covered up and it worked.
Another time, they came looking, also for deserters.
And they found me with another group of kids-- kids-- young people.
We had a group of young people that we were together, and they were going to arrest me.
And I literally jumped.
I told the people, I says, I'm go-- I told them in Yiddish so that he wouldn't understand, to form a line and I'm going to run away.
They knew it was you, the Russians.
The Russians knew by that-- no, they came and they ask for paper.
I had no papers.
So they said come with me, and I told the kids, I said, just form a barrier between him and me, and I run away into the cornfields.
And I heard shots after me, and they-- naturally they missed me, but--
This was in the Kirghizia in 1942, between '42 and '43.
Yeah, oh yeah.
And I remember the second time when they came.
I was constantly on the run.
I had no papers, because at that point, like I said, they were a little more liberal with us, and we were allowed to do a little handling, a little dealing on the bazaar.
They used to have, once a week, they had a bazaar in the town square.
And most of our people were allowed to travel between cities and bring back purchases of different articles and materials.
In other words, in one place, they would buy leather and bring back sugar-- because the big problem in the Soviet Union was transportation.
They didn't know how to organize. hey They would have in one place, they would have tons of salt.
In the other place, tons of sugar, but hard to transport one to the other.
Our people were able to do that and [INAUDIBLE].
So from that point on, we were able to wheel and deal, so to speak, in different products, merchandise.
It's not that I wanted, I had no choice, because they wanted me to be in the labor front, and I didn't want to.
So once I run away from them, I had no papers.
I was, yeah, with family or whenever we heard they're coming to look for-- not just for me, I mean personally, but for-- I wasn't the only one to run away.
So they were always looking for the guys that were neither in the army or in the labor front.
No.
Only among our-- well, we managed to bribe some of the [INAUDIBLE] in a local level to look away, in other words, and not bother.
A couple years.
I was illegal from that point on, which is till 1945 or the beginning of '46.
OK.
During that time, we found out that our father was released from prison over there, because he was totally emaciated.
And my brother Sam and my sister went to visit him.
And the merchandise that they had was some fruit from the south, which was like gold over-- up north.
They were able to, for an apple or a pear or an orange or something like that, they-- they could make their way through.
Eventually, they managed to get him released, and-- I mean, he was released, and to get him a permit to travel to join the family.
This was in 1944?
Late '43 or '44.
Yeah, we stayed till the-- till the end of the war.
Yeah, we heard news.
We heard the progress that the Russian armies made.
We also heard a lot of the American advances.
Because all of a sudden, we had a lot of goods from the United States that filtered down over their, clothing.
These were intended for the Polish people, Polish citizens, I should say.
And whether they filtered down to us or not, but it was available, and it started coming down.
So that by the end of the war, we were allowed to return-- repatriate back to Poland.
The only problem is, I have no papers.
In order to travel in the-- in the Soviet Union, you gotta have a permit.
Somehow or other, my father was able to-- by that time, he was well, and he was able to negotiate with the local [INAUDIBLE], the chief, to give me a special permit to get out.
And so that's right, all you had to do is schmear them.
And by the time we got back to Poland-- well, first we were on the train all away from [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Belovodsk to Moscow.
It was the Trans-Siberian train-- not the Trans-Siberian, the southern train.
Anyway, we got to Moscow.
From Moscow, we got to Lviv, excuse me, which is Lemberg, and we stayed there a few weeks.
In the meantime--
Obviously, not-- not so terribly bad, because it doesn't stand out in my mind as to--
No.
No.
No.

This is why I say when we got back from Lviv, we got into Poland, we came into Selmentsi, it's the border town for Poland and the Ukraine, and the first greeting we got from the Pollacks was, dirty Jews, you survived?
Hitler didn't get you?
And this was our greetings.
And at that time, my brother--
Well, they heard or they saw, I guess.
We weren't typically Polish.
So at that time, my brother Sam, who by the way got married in Siberia to his fiancee.
My father gave him [INAUDIBLE].
He married them, and--
And that's the only wedding in the camp.
And I think we got an extra ration of food at the time to celebrate.
So when we got back to Poland, and my brother Sam heard the story, he just says, I'm not going to stay in Poland.
And he went right through to-- to Germany and to a DP camp in [INAUDIBLE], near Munich.
No, not at that time.
But my father and I and-- by the way, my sister got married in Kirghizia, in Belovodsk.
And so she was pregnant at the time.
So the five of us, my father, mother, and my sister and her husband and I, went to Lodz, L-O-D-Z, in Poland.
Because Warsaw was completely demolished.
We did.
My father and I went from Lodz-- we went back to Poland to see what's happening.
And when we got there, it was leveled off, our neighborhood, completely, including the Jewish ghetto area.
We were just on the outskirts.
There wasn't a single brick that was whole.
It was all broken up.
And we managed to see a neighbor or ours--
In Warsaw.
In Warsaw.
When we went to look for--
In '46, it was early, yeah, about May.
May.
Yeah.
Maybe April or May.
And the greeting we got-- this is supposedly a good neighbor, he says, Sender, you survived?
You're still alive?
And all that-- Hitler didn't get you?
And father said, this is it.
We're not going to stay in Lodz.
We decided we want to go to a DP camp in Germany.
So we managed-- he got a smuggler to take us across the border.
And so we packed up our things, whatever we had-- Oh, my sister gave birth to my nephew in Lodz, and he was an infant.
And we went to smuggle across the border.
And as soon as we got across, we got arrested by the German police, put in a prison camp.
And they stripped everything that we had and whatever valuables we still had and sent us back to Poland and-- and to near [INAUDIBLE], there was a small town, Walbrzych.
W-A-L-B-R-Z-Y-C-H.
Walbrzych, Near Breslau or [INAUDIBLE].
And so at that point, I said, we're not going to stay here either.
And I joined the-- a group of kids that were going to Palestine.
The Aliyah Bet.
I don't know if you've heard of the Aliyah Bet, the illegal immigration to Palestine.
No.
That was a general-- I guess they-- they had some people from [?
Haganad ?] that came down to transport mainly youth and younger kids also.
And so we went on this trip.
And we got through from Poland, through Czechoslovakia, and we stopped in Bratislava.
Somehow they had it prearranged that we were Greeks.
So they said don't speak any German.
Speak Hebrew.
We didn't know Hebrew so-- no.
He says speak Greek.
So we used Hebrew words, you know.
And we got as far as Vienna-- in Vienna, Austria.
And we were joined by another group over there.
And then we continued on to Salzburg, Austria, which is near the border of Germany.
And at that time, I knew that my brother was in Munich or near Munich-- [INAUDIBLE]-- and I decided to join him.
And--
I don't know why.
I guess things weren't so good, but I wanted to be with my brother, Sam.
And so I smuggled across the border myself.
The American army was over there.
I tried once, and they stopped me.
Go back.
So I went around the other way, and I managed to get across.
And I came to my brother's place.
He was in the U-N-R-A, UNRA camp.
And-- Want to stop?
And they--
He was surprised to see me.
I was all by myself.
And he was a little bit of a [INAUDIBLE] there, because he was one of the earlier groups.
And he says, how do you like to go to America?
I said, I'd like to.
So he was able to change my papers.
He heard that the United States Committee for Children was taking children to the United States.
And you had to be under 18, and--
So he managed to change my age, because I was over 18, and declare me as an orphan.
And that's how I came to United States on the earliest part of 1946, in December 1946.
